Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic. I work part-time to help pay for things since my family can't support me much financially, so I had to squeeze in studying whenever I could. I'd usually grab a few hours here and there, maybe late at night or between shifts. I tried to keep things organized with a planner, but life kept getting in the way. I'd say I averaged about 16 hours a week, but those hours were scattered all over the place.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was happening that week. If I had a deadline coming up, I'd cram everything in at once and basically live on coffee for a few days. When things were calmer, I'd try to spread it out more evenly, but that was rare. Work schedules changed constantly, so I couldn't really commit to studying at the same times each week even if I wanted to.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something due soon or when I needed to check if any new assignments had been posted. Sometimes I'd get an email notification that reminded me to check in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around - I went in with a purpose because my time was limited. If I had a few minutes before work, I'd quickly check what needed to be done.
Q4: I wouldn't say the LMS ever became a natural habit for me, honestly. There were a couple of weeks where I was logging in more regularly because I was trying to catch up on videos I'd missed. But it never felt automatic - it was always a conscious effort to remember to check it. With everything else going on, the LMS was just one more thing on my to-do list rather than something integrated into my daily routine.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could watch them at my own pace and rewind parts I didn't understand. Since I learn best by doing things hands-on, I appreciated when there were practical examples I could follow along with. I also found the assignment submission portal helpful because it was straightforward - upload and done. The resources that broke things down step by step worked best for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely. I just didn't have time to sit there and write posts when I could be actually studying the material. The extra reading materials also got pushed aside a lot because I had to prioritize what was going to be on exams. Some of the supplementary videos felt like nice-to-haves rather than essentials, so those got skipped when I was short on time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I'd be tested on or if it contributed to my grade. I needed to see a clear connection between the effort I put in and the outcome. If something was going to take an hour but only marginally help me, I'd skip it. When you're juggling work and school with limited resources, you get really good at prioritizing what actually matters.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they got done no matter what. Optional stuff, even if it seemed interesting, always got bumped to the bottom of my list. There was a mental weight difference too - required activities stressed me out if I hadn't done them, but optional ones I could let go of without guilt.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and lecture slides to my phone so I could review them during my commute or breaks at work. I borrowed textbooks from the library instead of buying them, and I'd photocopy the important chapters. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate who had better notes than me. None of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I was studying hard but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd print out materials and highlight them at the coffee shop where I work during slow periods. I'd quiz myself using flashcards I made by hand. Sometimes I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then replay my own screen recording of it later because my internet at home isn't always reliable. The LMS only saw a fraction of my actual effort.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video or reading once, download or screenshot what I needed, and then work with it offline for days. My internet connection isn't great, so streaming videos multiple times wasn't practical. I'd rather have everything saved locally where I could access it anytime, even on the bus or during my lunch break at work.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my work increased my hours and I was exhausted all the time. I remember a few weeks where I just couldn't keep up and my attendance dropped badly. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me manage the stress better and get back on track. When I was running on empty, even opening the LMS felt overwhelming.
Q13: All of those things affected me at different points. Fatigue was probably the biggest one - after a long shift, the last thing I wanted to do was log in and watch a lecture. Confusion about assignments sometimes made me avoid them until the last minute because I didn't know where to start. When my work schedule conflicted with live sessions, I'd miss them and then struggle to catch up on my own.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be logged in watching a video but my mind was completely elsewhere, thinking about bills or work drama. I'd get to the end and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. There were times I'd scroll through materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really learning. The engagement was physical but not mental.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments that felt overwhelming or confusing. If I didn't understand the instructions clearly, I'd put it off hoping it would make more sense later - which usually just made things worse. Sometimes I avoided things because I was scared of failing at them. It wasn't laziness; it was more like a protective mechanism when I was already stretched thin.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd be pretty quiet on the platform and then suddenly log in a bunch right before something was due. It wasn't ideal, but that pressure was what got me moving. Without a deadline, things would drift indefinitely. I became very good at calculating exactly how much time I needed to complete something at the last minute.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with the next assignment. If I got a good score on something, I'd feel like my efforts were worth it and I'd be more likely to put in effort again. Negative feedback sometimes made me want to avoid that type of assignment in the future, though I tried not to let it get to me too much.
Q18: The structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. But the pace sometimes felt relentless when I was dealing with other life stuff. I appreciated when there was some flexibility built in, but I struggled when everything piled up at once. The structure worked when I could keep up with it.
Q19: The asynchronous content was a lifesaver because I could fit it around my work schedule. That made engagement possible when it otherwise wouldn't have been. What made it harder was when participation in discussions was required - I just couldn't commit to being online at specific times regularly. Also, some of the resources assumed we had reliable internet and plenty of time, which wasn't my reality.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete the work. Maybe they're watching videos and reading materials but can't translate that into finished assignments due to confusion or time constraints. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that let them browse but not focus enough to produce quality work. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: This actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was low, but I still passed with decent scores because I was doing a lot of work offline. I'd study from downloaded materials, borrowed books, and notes from peers. The LMS only captured a small part of my learning process. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but that wasn't the case at all.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental effort of working through problems on paper. They also can't capture your circumstances - whether you were stressed, sick, working, or dealing with family stuff. The data is just the surface.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in genuine effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present when you're studying, asking questions when confused, and connecting what you learn to real life. For me, engagement also means finding ways to keep going even when things get hard. It's not about perfect attendance; it's about commitment.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the nights I stayed up studying after exhausting work shifts, or how I had to get creative with limited resources. It can't capture my determination to finish my degree and build a better life. The data sees my low engagement numbers, but it doesn't see my resilience.
